burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #61935571.

cross_connections.display_label: Connecticut Militia General who took him in at four
cross_connections.notes: Cyprian's father Nathaniel Hart No. 2 died October 13, 1773 when Cyprian was four. His childless uncle Gen. Selah Hart (whose only natural child Mary had died young) adopted him as heir to the General Hart homestead. Cyprian died March 1, 1806; Selah died fourteen weeks later on June 10, 1806.

cross_connections.display_label: Two Cyprian Harts, both adopted by Gen. Selah Hart
cross_connections.notes: H00562 (this entry) and H00568 both bore the name Cyprian Hart and both were documented as adopted heirs of General Selah Hart (H00273) — a striking naming + adoption coincidence.

narrative_blocks.header: Father dead at four, raised on uncle's homestead
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel Hart died at Kensington on October 13, 1773, his sixth son Cyprian was four years old. Widowed Martha Norton soon removed to Hartford, and the boy was taken in by his childless uncle General Selah Hart and raised on the General Hart homestead as adopted heir.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Lucy Hooker at twenty-three, both born 1772
narrative_blocks.body: On December 24, 1795 he married Lucy Hooker of the same Kensington parish, born four months and ten days after him in January 1772. They were second cousins through their shared great-grandfather, the Honorable John Hooker of Farmington. Six children arrived in nine years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died before the General who had raised him
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Kensington on March 1, 1806, aged thirty-seven, leaving his youngest daughter Emily nine months old. General Selah Hart followed him into the grave fourteen weeks later, on June 10, in the same house. By the adoption arrangement the General Hart estate passed through his nephew's six children rather than to Selah's collateral kin.

narrative_blocks.header: Two widows shared the house thirty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: After both husbands died in the spring of 1806, his widow Lucy Hooker Hart and his adoptive aunt Ruth Cole Hart kept the Kensington homestead together for the rest of their lives. Ruth died in the house in January 1844 at one hundred and one. Lucy lived another seven years alone, dying there in July 1851.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #61935571.

cross_connections.display_label: Connecticut Militia General who took him in at four
cross_connections.notes: Cyprian's father Nathaniel Hart No. 2 died October 13, 1773 when Cyprian was four. His childless uncle Gen. Selah Hart (whose only natural child Mary had died young) adopted him as heir to the General Hart homestead. Cyprian died March 1, 1806; Selah died fourteen weeks later on June 10, 1806.

cross_connections.display_label: Two Cyprian Harts, both adopted by Gen. Selah Hart
cross_connections.notes: H00562 (this entry) and H00568 both bore the name Cyprian Hart and both were documented as adopted heirs of General Selah Hart (H00273) — a striking naming + adoption coincidence.

narrative_blocks.header: Father dead at four, raised on uncle's homestead
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel Hart died at Kensington on October 13, 1773, his sixth son Cyprian was four years old. Widowed Martha Norton soon removed to Hartford, and the boy was taken in by his childless uncle General Selah Hart and raised on the General Hart homestead as adopted heir.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Lucy Hooker at twenty-three, both born 1772
narrative_blocks.body: On December 24, 1795 he married Lucy Hooker of the same Kensington parish, born four months and ten days after him in January 1772. They were second cousins through their shared great-grandfather, the Honorable John Hooker of Farmington. Six children arrived in nine years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died before the General who had raised him
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Kensington on March 1, 1806, aged thirty-seven, leaving his youngest daughter Emily nine months old. General Selah Hart followed him into the grave fourteen weeks later, on June 10, in the same house. By the adoption arrangement the General Hart estate passed through his nephew's six children rather than to Selah's collateral kin.

narrative_blocks.header: Two widows shared the house thirty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: After both husbands died in the spring of 1806, his widow Lucy Hooker Hart and his adoptive aunt Ruth Cole Hart kept the Kensington homestead together for the rest of their lives. Ruth died in the house in January 1844 at one hundred and one. Lucy lived another seven years alone, dying there in July 1851.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-widow in the General Hart house thirty-eight years
cross_connections.notes: After Cyprian Hart died March 1, 1806 and General Selah Hart died June 10, 1806 in the same house, Lucy (then 34) and Ruth Cole Hart (then 63) — wives of nephew and adoptive uncle — kept the General Hart homestead together for as long as they lived. Ruth died there January 15, 1844 at 101 years, 2 months, 16 days. Lucy lived another seven years alone, dying July 8, 1851 at 79. EH #537: 'his widow and the widow of his uncle Gen. Selah Hart occupied the General Hart house and property together as long as they lived.'

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #61935595.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-four with six young children
narrative_blocks.body: She lost her husband Cyprian Hart on March 1, 1806 and his adoptive uncle General Selah Hart fourteen weeks later on June 10. By the adoption arrangement she remained mistress of the General Hart homestead with her six children, the youngest only nine months old. She had all six baptized at Kensington church the following November.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-five years a widow, three children unmarried
narrative_blocks.body: Of her six children, three died unmarried before their forty-second year: Selah at forty-one in 1838, the physician Henry Cyprian at thirty in 1831, and Emily at twenty-nine in 1835. She herself died July 8, 1851 at Kensington aged seventy-nine — having outlived her husband by forty-five years and having shared the General Hart house with her co-widow Ruth Cole Hart for thirty-eight of them.

naming_inspiration.description: Eldest son named for his father's adoptive uncle General Selah Hart, in whose Kensington homestead he was raised from birth.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the General who adopted his father
narrative_blocks.body: Eldest son of Cyprian Hart and Lucy Hooker, born October 19, 1796 in the General Hart homestead at Kensington. His given name honored his father's adoptive uncle General Selah Hart, then sixty-four years old and living in the same house. He carried the General's name through forty-one unmarried years and died at Kensington on May 24, 1838.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground, Kensington CT. FindAGrave Memorial #15158620.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Wallingford captain as his third wife
narrative_blocks.body: On March 25, 1829 at Kensington she married Captain Albert Norton of Wallingford, son of Roger and Hannah Rice Norton. She was his third wife — his first Lucy Lee died in 1824, his second Rebecca Eddy in 1828, both buried at Kensington. Ruth was thirty-two; Albert was thirty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried first daughter, named the next after her
narrative_blocks.body: Her first daughter Isabella was born October 15, 1831 and died at Kensington on December 15, 1840, aged nine. Two and a half years later, on July 5, 1843, she bore a second daughter and named her Harriet Isabel — the middle name carrying the dead sister's name forward.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground, Kensington CT. FindAGrave Memorial #15158640.
South Burying Ground, Kensington CT. FindAGrave Memorial #15158613.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at nine, sister later named after her
narrative_blocks.body: Born October 15, 1831 at Kensington, eldest child of Capt. Albert Norton and Ruth Hart — her father was forty-two and her mother thirty-four. She died at Kensington on December 15, 1840 aged nine. Two and a half years later her parents named their second daughter Harriet Isabella, carrying her name forward.

bio.extra_names: EH #2832 records as 'Harriet Isabel'; FindAGrave and her own gravestone use 'Harriet Isabella'.
bio.photo_notes: Photo provided by Sam Hooker (project compiler).

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #12917851.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors her older sister Isabella Norton, who died at age nine three years before her birth.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for an older sister dead at nine
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 5, 1843 at Kensington, second daughter of Capt. Albert Norton and Ruth Hart. Her sister Isabella had died at Kensington on December 15, 1840 aged nine, and her parents gave her the middle name Isabella to carry it forward. Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy abbreviated it to 'Isabel' in EH 2832, but her gravestone and surviving records preserve the full form.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Civil War veteran and Meriden banker
narrative_blocks.body: She became the second wife of Edwin Howell Butler of Meriden, Civil War veteran of Company C of the Seventh Connecticut Volunteers and later president of Home National Bank. He had been previously married to Marie L. Brown, who died in 1874.

cross_connections.display_label: Inherited his homestead two generations after his death
cross_connections.notes: The General Hart homestead at Kensington descended through three generations: built and lived in by Gen. Selah Hart (d. 1806); held by his adopted nephew Cyprian Hart (Abigail's father) and his cohabiting widows Lucy Hooker and Ruth Cole Hart for thirty-eight years; passed to Abigail and her husband Oliver Moore upon her mother's death in 1851. Hart Genealogy explicitly identifies the property: 'where the late Oliver Moore lived.'

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited the General Hart homestead through marriage
narrative_blocks.body: On March 25, 1829 at Kensington she married Oliver Moore. The General Hart house at Kensington — built by her great-uncle General Selah Hart, held by her adopted-heir father Cyprian Hart from 1773 to 1806, then by her widowed mother and great-aunt Ruth Cole Hart together for thirty-eight years — became the Moore residence by the time of her own death in 1850. Three generations of Hart property descent ended at her household.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors his father Cyprian Hart, who died when Henry was five years old.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale Medical M.D. 1826; began practice in his native Kensington parish.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #62169513.

education.notes: M.D. 1826. Attended two courses of lectures per Yale Biographical Notices.

career.organization: Private practice, Kensington Parish, Berlin CT
career.notes: Started practice in his native parish immediately after receiving M.D. Died in practice March 28, 1831 age 30.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised by his widowed mother and adoptive great-aunt
narrative_blocks.body: Born January 30, 1801 at the General Hart homestead in Kensington, fifth child and younger son of Cyprian Hart and Lucy Hooker. His father died March 1, 1806 when Henry was five years and one month old. He was raised in the household of his widowed mother Lucy and her adoptive aunt Ruth Cole Hart, who together kept the homestead from 1806 onward.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Medical degree at twenty-five, physician at twenty-six
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in the Class of 1822 at age twenty-one, then attended two courses of lectures at the Yale Medical School and received the M.D. degree in 1826 in his twenty-fifth year. He began practice in his native Kensington parish almost immediately.

narrative_blocks.header: A man of high promise, dead at thirty
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine in Kensington for five years and died there on March 28, 1831, aged thirty, unmarried. The Yale Biographical Notices summary records him as regarded as a man of high promise. His sister Emily, also unmarried, would die at the same Kensington parish four years later at age twenty-nine.

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256921.

naming_inspiration.description: Given the full name of her mother Lucy Hooker Hart.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her mother; married into Blue Hills
narrative_blocks.body: Born November 14, 1803 at Kensington, fifth child and youngest daughter, given the full name of her mother Lucy Hooker Hart. On November 1, 1826 — two weeks before her twenty-third birthday — she married Sheldon Peck of Blue Hills, Kensington, son of Lemuel and Lydia Dickinson Peck, nine years her senior. Five children followed.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Meriden four months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Sheldon grew infirm and the couple removed from Kensington to Meriden, where he died July 31, 1872 aged seventy-eight. She survived him by three months and twelve days, dying at Meriden on November 12, 1872 aged sixty-nine. Both were buried at Walnut Grove Cemetery in Meriden, alongside her husband and the children who survived them.

bio.extra_names: FindAGrave Memorial #13256922 displays his name as 'Seldon Peck'; other sources use 'Sheldon Peck'.

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256922.

career.notes: Farmer at Blue Hills, Kensington. Removed to Meriden late in life due to infirmity.

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256919.

bio.extra_names: EH #2834 recorded only as 'Hattie G.' — FindAGrave and obituary record confirm Harriet Emily Peck Bradley, 'Hattie' as nickname.

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256929.

naming_inspiration.description: First name possibly honors her first cousin once removed Harriet Isabella Norton Butler (H02832, b. 1843) — but H02832 was born nine years AFTER Harriet Emily, so this naming pattern is reversed: H02832 may have been the one named partly after this elder Hooker-line cousin, OR both share an unidentified earlier Harriet ancestor in the Norton/Peck families. Naming research deferred.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Bradley & Hubbard co-founder, 1859 Meriden
narrative_blocks.body: On her twenty-fifth birthday year, 1859, she married Nathaniel Lyman Bradley of Meriden — co-founder, treasurer, and later president of the Bradley & Hubbard Manufacturing Company, the kerosene-lamp and metal-goods firm that grew to seven acres and 1,500 employees. Their only son Clarence Peck Bradley would succeed Nathaniel as president in 1915 and leave the firm's Meriden homestead and over a million dollars for the Bradley Home for the Elderly. She survived her husband by five years.

bio.extra_names: EH #2835 recorded as 'Henry H.' — FindAGrave confirms middle name Hart (his mother's maiden name).
bio.photo_notes: Photo provided by Sam Hooker (project compiler).

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256924.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hart' honors his mother Lucy Hooker Hart's maiden line — descended from Gen. Selah Hart's adopted nephew Cyprian Hart of Kensington.

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256920.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors his father Sheldon Peck of Blue Hills.

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #13256923.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died ten months after she was born
narrative_blocks.body: Born May 19, 1805 at Kensington, youngest of six children. Her father Cyprian Hart was already in failing health and died March 1, 1806 when she was nine months and eleven days old. She knew her father only as the children's memory of a deathbed.

narrative_blocks.header: Died unmarried at twenty-nine, like brother Henry Cyprian
narrative_blocks.body: She died at Kensington on April 3, 1835 aged twenty-nine, unmarried — four years and six days after her physician brother Henry Cyprian Hart had died in the same parish at age thirty, also unmarried. Of Cyprian and Lucy's six children, three died unmarried before forty-two: Selah at forty-one, Henry Cyprian at thirty, Emily at twenty-nine.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. John Hart (b. Apr 2, 1655, d. Nov 11, 1714)

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Mary Hooker (H00100, b.~1699); she had married Lieut. Samuel Hart in 1723.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

death.cause: Year only; month/day approximate per project convention. Source: 'died 1761 aged 63.'

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons served the Revolution differently
narrative_blocks.body: Her eldest son Selah rose to General and was captured by the British in 1776; Noadiah served as Lieutenant; and Asahel, Yale-educated and ordained as a minister, died in 1775 at age 33 before the war's outcome was known. Three brothers from one Hooker-Hart household, three paths through the Revolution.

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.
